name: [TEST FIXTURE] Wilderness Survival
sourceAttribution: AUTO-GENERATED TEST FIXTURE — placeholder content, not for production. Real authoring will replace this with cited content.

guides.title: Priorities of survival
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

In most environments, the priority order is: shelter, water, fire, food. You can survive only minutes without air, hours without shelter in extreme cold or heat, days without water, and weeks without food.

This order shifts. In a hot desert at midday, water is the immediate priority. In an arctic blizzard, shelter is. The general rule: protect against whatever will kill you fastest given the current conditions, then move down the list.

guides.title: Basic navigation without GPS
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Shadow stick: place an upright stick in the sun, mark the tip of its shadow, wait 15+ minutes, mark again. The line between marks runs west-east; the first mark is west.

In the Northern Hemisphere at night, find Polaris (north star) by extending the line of the two front stars of the Big Dipper's bowl ~5x. Polaris stays nearly fixed above true north.

Pace count: know how many of your double-steps cover 100 meters. Lets you estimate distance traveled on a heading.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Friction fire — bow drill basics
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder. Bow drill is hard. Practice before you need it.]

Need: a spindle (dry softwood ~8 inches), fireboard (flat softwood with a notch), bow (bent stick + cordage), socket (rock or hardwood with a divot for the spindle top), tinder bundle.

Apply downward pressure on the socket while sawing the bow rapidly. Friction creates a coal in the notch beneath the spindle. Transfer the coal carefully to the tinder bundle and blow gently until flame catches.
guides.sourceRef: [TEST FIXTURE] AUTO-GENERATED — no real source. Real authoring will replace this with a CDC/FEMA/NIH/etc citation.

guides.title: Universal edibility test — and why to avoid it
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder. Misidentified plants are a leading cause of foraging deaths. When in doubt, do not eat.]

The traditional test (separate plant parts, test for skin reaction, taste a tiny amount, wait 8 hours, etc.) takes a full day per plant and gives only weak protection against toxins that cause delayed harm (liver damage, kidney damage).

The safe rule for a few-day survival situation: don't eat anything you cannot positively identify. Hunger isn't fatal on day 3. Most poisonings are.

guides.title: Water from the environment
guides.content:
[TEST FIXTURE — placeholder.]

Dew: drag clean cloth through grass at dawn, wring into a container.

Transpiration bag: tie a clear plastic bag over a leafy non-toxic branch in sun. Water condenses inside over hours.

Solar still: dig a pit, place container in center, cover with plastic weighted in the middle. Water evaporates from soil and condenses on the plastic.

Snow: melt first, never eat directly (costs body heat). Filter or boil snow water from urban or industrial areas; pristine snow can still carry contaminants.
